questions.prompt:
Read: "Ali is 12, Sami is 14, and Nour is 15. Sami is taller than Ali, but Nour is the tallest of the three."
Which sentence is TRUE?

options.text: Ali is the oldest of the three.

questions.explanation: If Nour is the tallest of all three, then Nour is taller than Sami, so (b) is true. (a) is false — Ali is the youngest, not the oldest; (c) reverses the text (Sami is taller than Ali); and (d) is false — Ali is the youngest at 12.

options.text: This is the most interesting book I have read.
Winter is colder than autumn here.
He runs more faster than everyone in his team.
She is the best student in the school.

questions.explanation: The error is (c): fast is a short adjective, so the comparative is just faster than — "more faster" double-marks it (more + -er). (a) uses the most for a long adjective, (b) the -er comparative with than, and (d) the irregular superlative the best — all correct.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The blue car is expensive, the red car is ___, but the black car is ___ of all."
questions.explanation: Expensive is a long adjective: comparative more expensive (the red car vs the blue), superlative the most expensive (top of all). Only (b) is right. "expensiver"/"the expensivest" wrongly add endings to a long adjective, and (d) swaps the comparative and superlative.

options.text: My house is bigger than yours, but theirs is the biggest.
My house is more big than yours, but theirs is the biggest.
My house is bigger than yours, but theirs is the most big.
My house is more bigger than yours, but theirs is the biggest.

questions.explanation: Big is a short adjective: it doubles the g for both forms — comparative bigger than, superlative the biggest. Only (a) has both. (b) uses "more big", (c) uses "the most big", and (d) double-marks the comparative with "more bigger".

questions.prompt: Complete: "The library is ___ from here than the school, so let's go to the school first."
questions.explanation: Far is irregular: its comparative is further (or farther), used here with than to compare two distances: further from here than the school. "farer"/"more far" invent regular forms that don't exist, and "the furthest" is the superlative, which doesn't take than.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The phone shop sells three models. The basic one is the cheapest. The pro model is more expensive than the basic one, and the ultra model costs even more than the pro."
Which model is the most expensive?
questions.explanation: The ultra costs more than the pro, and the pro costs more than the basic, so the ultra model is the most expensive (c). The basic is the cheapest (a is wrong), the pro is in the middle (b), and the three prices are clearly different, so (d) is false.
